items.transcript: This is your sign to be a little toxic in your next job interview. Let me explain. From 2022 to 2025, there was a frenzy of AI investment. And now in the great year of our Lord 2026, it's time for that to start paying off. Companies need to deliver. And you can use this to your advantage. All those layoffs they did to replace people with AI, all those AI demos they showed to clients promising new features and products, AI is so powerful, they said. And we have the solutions. Well, time's up. Time to start delivering. AI's gotta start making that money. So every company right now has top down initiatives. So if you go into any job interview right now and you bring up these initiatives, gold, money, five stars. But Erin, how do I even figure out what these initiatives are? One word, LinkedIn. LinkedIn is your secret. Every CEO of every company posts on LinkedIn, like a lot. It's like they're being held at gunpoint. So go to LinkedIn, type in the company name, read their posts. What's top of mind for the leadership? What's the strategy? What's the focus? What's the anxiety? And use that against them. So poke around, see what's going on, leave a couple comments. Really love this angle, Bob. Shelly, great points all around. You lurk. Oh, this B2B SaaS company is moving their focus from enterprise to SMBs? Interesting. You go into the interview, see I noticed the CEO recently posted about this on LinkedIn. Is this a major initiative right now? And they say, yes, actually it is. You say, well, with my previous experience in X, I'd be more than equipped to assist with that. Boom, done, hired. All companies have anxiety right now. So if you know how to use that against them, like a toxic little troll, in a truthful and honest way, of course. You got this. And follow for more.

items.caption:
This video is over 3 minutes so apparently IG won’t show it to anyone lol but there’s no quicker way to truncate a nuanced 32-page study & the misinformation was driving me nuts 😅 — I read the study, here’s what you need to know! And I def encourage you to read the study for yourself 🫶🏼

You got this 👏 #stanford #hiring #ai
items.transcript: The Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence just released a study that is going completely viral because it's exposing the secret AI hiring algorithm that gives people a resume score that follows them around from company to company and is causing people to get mass auto-rejected before the companies even see their application. Just kidding. The study literally doesn't say that at all. And anybody who's making videos saying that clearly just hasn't read the study and is just trying to go viral. The study barely even mentions resumes. I know that because I actually read it. So let me tell you what it actually says and what it means for your job search. If we haven't met before, hi, I'm Erin, also known as a vice with Erin and the founder of Stupid Fish. So this was a huge study. They followed 4 million people as they submitted 3.4 million job applications to 1,700 jobs across 11 sectors. But the data was empirical, meaning they weren't running experiments. The entire study is based on data from one company, which we're going to talk about later. So when you apply for a job online, most of the time your application is first going to be screened by a third-party software. This is because applying to jobs has become much, much easier. With things like quick apply, mass apply, globalization, remote work, companies are getting inundated with applications. And so they use this third-party software to help them organize applications. This software has been around for decades and honestly, it's always been problematic. Now, the interesting thing is that there are a handful of companies that dominate the space. Companies like Workday, Greenhouse, Bamboo. And just like every other company on earth, they're building in AI tools. However, every company that uses this software sets it up differently. So that doesn't mean just because they're using the software, they're using AI. You follow me? It also doesn't mean that this software is actually AI. Everybody thinks everything is AI now. People will be like, oh, my dishwasher has AI. And I'm like, that's not AI, Brad. That's Bluetooth. It's been around for decades. I digress. Also, this study didn't even look at any of those companies. They didn't have access to their data. The entire study is based around one company called Pymetrics. It's a talent platform that some companies might use to assess candidates by having them participate in gameplay. Basically using little games to assess their personality type and stuff. So what the study actually found is that candidates who apply to jobs via Pymetrics complete the assessment. They have those results impact all their jobs they apply to using Pymetrics via this recommend versus do not recommend feature that they offer. That's it. Is it problematic if it doesn't work? Yeah. Could other software companies be practicing something similar? We don't know. We don't have insight into that. And that is why these researchers' takeaway and recommendation is not stop using AI. It's we need more transparency into what is going on. Now, something the study did find that I don't hear anybody talking about, yet it's the major takeaway from the study, is a pattern of racial discrimination. The study found that 26% of black applicants and 15% of Asian applicants apply to at least one position where the algorithm recommended them at a lower rate than the most favorite group, which is usually white applicants. The crazy thing is when you average all the data out, you don't see the racial bias. However, what these researchers did is they looked at it role by role and they found something really interesting. A black person may be more likely to be recommended for a warehouse job that already employs a large amount of black people, while a white person may be higher recommended to work at a bank if they already employed more white people. This is problematic. This is something we need to be talking about. Hiring is so inefficient and we have to figure out solutions. So my practical advice in the meantime, one, apply to smaller companies. They can't afford to use these fancy software, so they just review things manually. Two, network. The easiest way to get around all this computer slop mess is just to talk directly to a human being. You got this and follow for more.

items.caption:
#stitch with the lovely @askcatgpt ✨ 3 tips on how to be more direct ~without~ being rude! 

PSA in case no one told you: you do NOT have to change your personality in order to become a direct person. 

Directness comes in all shapes and sizes. But remember: being clear is literally the nicest thing you can do for other people 🙌

You got this 👏 #careeradvice #communication 

💬 Don’t have my book yet!? Tragedy!! Type “BOOK” & I’ll send you the link (I read the audiobook too 🎙️)
items.transcript: but TBD on budget. So I guess like a level of interest so far, like you haven't engaged there? If it's not paid, I just, I don't have time. I'm going to be so busy. Yeah, respectfully. They're great girls. I love their team, but they're not good enough friends to be doing favors. If you're someone who's trying to learn how to be direct without being a dick, Hi, I'm Erin. I literally wrote an entire book on this. So here are three things that you can start trying today. Number one, change your mindset around directness. Like Brene Brown says, clear is kind and unclear is unkind. People-pleasing is actually super rude because you're wasting people's most precious resource, time. And so the more direct and clear you can be about what you want and need, the more you're respectful of other people's time, which is actually the kindest thing that you can do for other people. Number two, be soft on the person, but firm on the issue. There's what you say, and then there's how you say it. This is why my number one tip for negotiating is to smile. Be soft in tone, but firm in words. Let's say a coworker is trying to get you to cover their shift on Saturday. You could say, I don't really know, I'm not sure, but let me check my calendar and I can let you know. Or, oh, I'm unavailable on Saturday, but I hope you find someone. You do not have to be rude to be direct. Just a little smile can go a long way. And number three, know your audience. Treat directness like a dial. It's not black or white. For example, in this video, Kat is clearly talking about a third party that is not present in the conversation. She's talking to an intermediary so she can get away with being more blunt and curt. She knows her audience. However, if she was talking directly to the party that she is rejecting, I would recommend using this template from the Harvard Program on Negotiating. Yes, no, yes. And that could sound something like, that sounds like a really cool event. Unfortunately, I'm unable to accommodate unpaid opportunities at this time, but please keep me in mind in the future, and I hope it's a huge success. It's kind of like giving your dog a pill wrapped in cheese. Because your rejection is so positive in tone, it's impossible to label you as a diva. You got this, and follow for more.

items.transcript: Papas!

items.caption:
I’ve said it before and I’ll say it again 👩🏼‍💻📣

AI is not causing mass layoffs. 
It’s not the reason hiring is sluggish. 

The economy is just really bad rn! 

AI 100% has an impact on the current job market (obvi!) as there is mass demand for re/upskilling — but it’s become a scapegoat that gives the people actually responsible for the lackluster job market a huge pass. 

The reason the job market sucks is because the economy sucks. 

AI isn’t there (yet). It’s not an overnight disruption. It will slowly change jobs, some quicker than others, but we are far away from it replacing human workers 1:1. 

Tech is always evolving. They did surgery on a grape!

Quick q - have you had a coworker laid off & seen the company 100% replace their *entire job* with AI overnight? (Not having company *claim* they did, but actually witness it firsthand in your department?) Anyone? Anyone, please! 

The economy sucks for many reasons, but I believe a driving factor is a general sense of unpredictability due to extremely poor leadership. Economies thrive under strong leadership with clear strategy that they follow through on. 

When policies are chaotic & constantly volatile, companies can’t plan for the future. 
When companies feel they can’t plan for the future, they don’t take risks. 
When they don’t take risks, they don’t grow. 
When they don’t grow, they stop hiring. 
That’s it!

AI is definitely shaking things up, but not in the way the powers at be want you to think it is. AI has a ton of potential, but at this time, very little payoff. The s-curve is s-curving!

AI is a very convenient scapegoat and it’s finally catching up. 2026 is the year of the great payoff! So let’s get realistic about AI, ok entire job market? Stop laying people off “because of AI” and just be honest 🫶 the economy is not thriving + we’ve got to get a grip on reality to make the hiring market healthy again. 

🫶🏼 erin

(ps friendly reminder that i did not invent AI and I do not control the job market / economy. I do a ton of research and do my best to give realistic advice according to the data I have access to. I’m on your side ❤️)

Here’s what you can say next time they ask you about your salary expectations ✍🏼

💬 Comment “SALARY101” for my free salary negotiation guide

items.transcript: Hey, did you enjoy your three-day weekend? Yeah, I had fun with my friends. Are you feeling refreshed and reinvigorated for work tomorrow? No, I don't feel refreshed or reinvigorated or rejuvenated or relaxed. There's a lot of hours. I feel like I don't want to go to work tomorrow. This is way worse than the Sunday scaries. Ah, the post-holiday blues. The what? The post-holiday blues. Also called post-vacation syndrome. It's an international phenomenon that psychologists have been writing about since the 1950s. Basically, after your time off, your nervous system has to re-acclimate to the demands of your normal life. So, the bigger the gap between your vacation life and your normal life, the worse the dip's gonna feel. Well, what am I supposed to do? This is actually a good thing. This is the universe's way of screaming at you to make a change. This confusion that you're feeling just means that you're on the cusp of a breakthrough. Exciting! Okay, so can you just, like, tell me what to do? I mean, I am so stuck, I don't even know where to start. Wait, have you not tried Stupid Fish yet? What's that? Uh, you have to try this. It's this new app that we just released for people in your exact situation. Like, you know there's better opportunity out there, but you just don't know how to find it? Stupid Fish. You do the initial assessment where we ask you some, like, deep questions about your life. Then, we cross-reference that with your skills and job market demand and show you new opportunities, some of which you might have never even considered before. Oh, wow, this is amazing. And would you look at that? You made the initial assessment and results 100% free for everyone. That is so cool of you to do that, Erin. You got this. And this is just the beginning.

items.transcript: How to answer one of the trickiest job interview questions, what are your salary expectations? Great, so we just have one last question for you here. What are your salary expectations? Um, I don't know, don't care. Well, I make $45,000 a year now, so anything better than that would be great. I mean, in a perfect world I'd get like $60,000 a year. Stop. Don't answer this question directly. This question is tricky. If you say too low of a number, you risk them lowballing you. And if you say too high of a number, you can risk them writing you off as too expensive. Try this. Great, so we just have one last question for you here. Um, what are your salary expectations for this role? Thank you so much for bringing that up. I would love to know the approved salary range for the position. Oh, well we don't really have a set range, you know, it depends on the candidate. Got it. Well, my salary range is flexible, but I'd like to learn more about the specifics of the role before giving out a solid number. Well, could you just give me a range? I just need to notate here. Well, I can tell you that I'm currently interviewing for roles that are in the $65,000 to $95,000 range, and I'm flexible on salary depending on other elements of the compensation package. You got this! Follow for more.

items.caption: Financial literacy 101 ✨💸
items.transcript: Generational wealth isn't just passed down through money, it's also passed down through knowledge. So here is one basic money rule that rich parents teach their kids. In the 2021-2022 season, the average NBA player made $8 million. Considering the fact the average NBA career is four to five years, that means that the average NBA player is making $30 to $40 million in their career. Definitely enough to last you the rest of your life, right? Well, in reality, 60 to 65 percent more than half of NBA players go completely broke within five years of retirement. So how does this happen? They don't follow the first rule of financial literacy, and that is to live below your means. Spend less than you make and invest the difference in income-generating assets. Because it doesn't matter how much money you're making, if you spend 100 percent of $5 or $5 million, you still end up with $0. And when it comes to living below your means, financially literate people focus less on the below and more on the means. They worry less about cutting costs and more about increasing income. And this is why getting rich and staying rich are two entirely different things. You got this and follow for more.
